petitioner_background: The petitioner is a trek and expedition guide to mountain climbers, specifically a Nepali mountain climber who intends to work as a trek and expedition leader. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). He has summited Mt. Everest six times and has worked as a mountain guide, including employment with an expedition company from 2004 through 2014 guiding clients on treks in Nepal and Tibet.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed membership in an association (name redacted), asserting it only grants membership to those who have reached the summit of Mt. Everest at least once. He submitted a webpage from the association stating it 'is an association of [climbers]' and that 'The Board of Committee members thus includes all the veteran Nepali climbers.' He also submitted a letter from the president of the association confirming the petitioner has summited Mt. Everest six times, in addition to other treks, and is registered as a member. As evidence that the president is an expert, he submitted documentation from her website discussing her mountain climbing achievements and noting she has been president of the association since 2015.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion because the record did not establish that his membership was based on his achievements as an expedition guide (the field for which classification was sought), nor did it establish that national or international experts judged his achievements for admission. The Director noted the record did not contain the bylaws or constitution of the association with details about the membership process and eligibility. While the president was shown to be an experienced mountain climber, the evidence did not demonstrate she is responsible for reviewing all membership applications. The evidence appeared to show that anyone who has climbed Mt. Everest is eligible for membership, which would include mountain guides as well as mountain climbers generally. No new evidence of admission requirements or process was submitted on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an article from the publication Golden Transcript about his work in the field. He also submitted a blog article from alanarnette.com discussing the keynote speaker at an annual dinner in 2015, which was followed by a panel discussion that included the petitioner.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. The Golden Transcript appears to be based in Golden, Colorado, but the record did not demonstrate the extent of its readership, so it was not established to be a professional or major trade publication or major media. For the alanarnette.com blog article, beyond identifying the petitioner as a panel participant, the article did not directly reference him again and focused on the issues discussed — thus it was not about him or his work in the field. Additionally, the record did not establish that the blog constitutes a professional or major trade publication or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters regarding his contributions, including a letter from an expedition guide who worked with him for more than 12 years stating that '[N]ot everyone can do the dangerous and technical job of rope fixing, but I can always count on [the Petitioner]. He has the technical climbing skills, the knowledge of the mountain and the strength (both physical and mental) to be trusted with setting the ropes and routes in a sound and safe manner. The climbers tasked with setting the ropes battle steep inclines to anchor the ropes so that up to 600 climbers may use them to navigate the treacherous terrain of Mt. Everest.' The record also contained many letters from climbers who had climbed Mt. Everest with the petitioner explaining heroic rescue efforts he undertook during the 2014 incident and the 2015 earthquake on Mt. Everest.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged the petitioner's expertise but found his contributions pertained to specific expeditions on Mt. Everest, not to the field as a whole. The petitioner had not demonstrated that his rope fixing work had major significance in the field. While the AAO recognized the personal impact he had in rescuing others on Mt. Everest during the 2014 incident and 2015 earthquake, the record did not demonstrate that these were original contributions of major significance throughout the field. No new evidence of the petitioner's original contributions was submitted on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed he performed a leading or critical role as a mountain guide for an expedition company. Initially, he submitted a letter written by the Managing Director of a different entity (not the claimed employer). On motion, he submitted a letter from the co-owner and program director of the company, stating that the company employed the petitioner from 2004 through 2014 as a mountain guide on treks in Nepal and Tibet, that he 'has played a critical role on many [] climbs, and has made significant contributions to the field by successfully and safely guiding many climbers on their high-altitude adventures,' that he 'has personally guided [] clients to the summit of [] on 6 expeditions,' and that 'he is one of our top guides in Nepal[,] and has played a critical role on many [] climbs.' The petitioner also claimed that National Geographic ranked the company as a top guide service, supported by a page from the company's own website.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the Director's favorable conclusion on this criterion. First, the evidence did not establish that the petitioner performed a leading or critical role for the organization as claimed — the record did not establish that his work on the expeditions was in a leadership role or one critical to the success of the organization. The AAO noted that repeating the language of the statute or regulations does not satisfy the petitioner's burden of proof, citing Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd v. Sava and Avyr Associates, Inc. v. Meissner. Second, the petitioner did not provide sufficient evidence to establish that the company had a distinguished reputation. While he claimed National Geographic had ranked the company highly, the only supporting evidence was a page from the company's own website; he did not submit evidence from National Geographic or other objective evidence to support this conclusion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal on de novo review because the petitioner did not submit the required initial evidence of either a qualifying one-time achievement, or documents meeting at least three of the ten criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x), or comparable evidence establishing eligibility. The AAO withdrew the Director's finding that the leading or critical role criterion had been met, leaving the petitioner with zero established criteria. The AAO also rejected the petitioner's comparable evidence argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), finding he had not demonstrated that the regulatory criteria do not readily apply to his occupation, noting the petitioner undermined his own argument regarding published material by citing articles on Sherpa mountaineers and a film. Because initial evidence requirements were not met, the AAO did not need to fully address the totality of materials in a final merits determination, but advised that it reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding of sustained acclaim and recognition required for the classification.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd, 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
Avyr Associates, Inc. v. Meissner, 1997 WL 188942 (S.D.N.Y.)

USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions; Revisions to the Adjudicator's Field Manual (AFM) Chapter 22.2, AFM Update ADJ 1-14 (Dec. 22, 2010)
